nativeRange.habitat: Found on lagoon and seaward reefs, living and feeding close to the reef structure at depths of roughly 8 to 35 m.

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer of macroalgae and microalgae. Generally advised to offer dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week, supplemented with high-quality herbivore pellet or flake foods. Has been documented accepting meaty protein foods (mysis, krill, enriched frozen preparations) alongside other tank inhabitants, but vegetable matter should dominate the diet to support immune function and reduce aggression.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Rachel Crane, 1321x1150. In-situ reef shot of a jet-black adult showing the elongated snout and diagnostic white caudal-peduncle spot; sharp, whole fish in frame.

summary: A jet-black Zebrasoma surgeonfish from the remote Eastern Central Pacific, recognized by its elongated snout, blue pectoral fins, and white peduncle spot. Generally considered reef-safe and best kept singly in spacious systems.
careNotes:
The Black Longnose Tang (Zebrasoma rostratum) is a striking, almost entirely jet-black surgeonfish distinguished by its notably elongated snout, blue pectoral fins, and a small white spot at the caudal peduncle near the scalpels. Adults typically reach about 21–23 cm in length.

This species is endemic to the remote Eastern Central Pacific, occurring around the Line, Marquesas, Society and Tuamotu Islands, extending to the Pitcairn Group. It inhabits lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 8–35 m. Following the closure of Hawaii's commercial aquarium fishery in 2020, specimens in the trade are generally sourced from the Christmas (Kiritimati) Island fishery in Kiribati.

Water quality should be stable and well-oxygenated. Conditions generally advised are around 23–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025, and dKH 8–12. A reef-style aquarium of 680 L or more with plenty of open swimming space is generally recommended, with a tank length of around 180 cm providing this active swimmer adequate territory. The species tends to use rockwork crevices for shelter and grazes algae from live rock.

Like other Zebrasoma species, the Black Longnose Tang tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped surgeonfish. It is best kept as the sole tang in a system and introduced last to an established tank. It has generally been reported to coexist peaceably with most non-surgeonfish reef inhabitants when given adequate space.

All surgeonfish possess paired sharp caudal-peduncle blades (called scalpels) that are used in defense. These are not venomous but can inflict serious lacerations on handlers and tankmates — care should be taken during transfers, and a tang should never be handled bare-handed or moved by net alone without accounting for the spines. Like most tangs, this species tends to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE), a condition marked by pitting and discoloration along the head and flanks, when kept in poor water quality or fed a diet lacking in vitamins and algae.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been documented in available sources. Zebrasoma surgeonfish are broadcast spawners in the wild, and successful aquarium reproduction is considered highly unlikely given the tank volumes and pelagic larval stages involved.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), max size (21 cm TL), depth (8–35 m), temperature range (23–28°C), native distribution (Line, Marquesas, Society, Tuamotu Islands to Pitcairn Group), and benthopelagic reef habitat. IUCN status: Data Deficient (2010 assessment).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (21 cm), reef-safe status, herbivorous algae-grazing diet, and Eastern Central Pacific range. Minimum tank cited as 300 L was substantially lower than other sources; not used per tang sizing rule. Reported salinity ~SG 1.026.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Black Longnose Tang has not been documented. Like other Zebrasoma surgeonfish, it spawns by releasing eggs and sperm into open water in the wild — a strategy that requires vast space and produces larvae with a lengthy pelagic phase, making aquarium reproduction highly unlikely.
